title: Houthis Declare Saudi Naval Blockade, Trump Tightens Critical Mineral Supply Chains, and EU Suspends Methane Law
subhead: Yemen's Houthis announced an immediate maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia through Bab el-Mandeb, opening a second chokepoint front alongside the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, Trump signed an executive order squeezing Chinese minerals from defense contracts, and the EU suspended its own methane law for three years, citing energy supply fears.

sections.heading: Houthis Declare Naval Blockade on Saudi Arabia at Bab el-Mandeb
sections.body:
Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi rebels announced an immediate maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia on Monday, declaring that the Bab el-Mandeb Strait would be closed to Saudi shipping. Houthi military spokesperson Yahya Saree called it 'an equation of an eye for an eye,' citing Saudi Arabia's decade-long blockade of Yemen and a recent strike on Sanaa International Airport. The deputy head of the Houthi media office, Nasruddin Amer, said the strait would be closed in response to what he called Saudi Arabia's 'unjust blockade on Yemenis for over 10 years.'

Bab el-Mandeb handles roughly 12% of global trade and a quarter of the world's container traffic via the Suez Canal. Saudi Arabia's military spokesperson, Maj. Gen. Turki al-Malki, said all Houthi threats against transiting vessels would be dealt with 'swiftly and firmly,' calling the threats 'acts of maritime piracy.' Rystad Energy estimated that about 2.5 million barrels per day of Saudi crude flowing through the East-West Pipeline to the Red Sea could now be at risk — the same pipeline Riyadh has relied on as a bypass for Hormuz disruptions.

The move escalates an already dangerous situation: Saudi-Houthi tensions had flared days earlier when Saudi forces reportedly struck Sanaa airport during a flight carrying Houthi leaders returning from Ayatollah Khamenei's funeral in Iran. The Houthis responded by launching missiles and drones at Abha International Airport in the most significant Saudi-Houthi confrontation in years.
sections.whyItMatters: With Hormuz already constricted, a second major chokepoint under active threat fundamentally changes the energy transit map. Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline, its Hormuz bypass, now exits into a Red Sea where its own shipping faces a Houthi embargo. Two of the world's three most critical oil passages are simultaneously contested.

sections.heading: Oil Dips as Ceasefire Proposal Offsets Houthi Escalation
sections.body:
Brent crude slipped 0.38% to $88.88 per barrel in early Asian trading on Tuesday, down from Monday's intraday high of $91.42. WTI traded nearly unchanged at $82.53. The pullback came after a senior Iranian official told Reuters that Tehran had received a proposal from mediators for a 10-day ceasefire aimed at reviving the interim deal reached last month. Oil had settled more than 1% higher on Monday as the Houthi blockade announcement initially spooked markets.

ANZ analysts noted that 'the supply narrative has become more bearish,' with Strait of Hormuz transit volumes falling to single digits. Gulf countries had boosted crude and condensate exports in the first half of July to their highest since before the war began in late February, but flows through Hormuz are slowing as fighting escalates. Meanwhile, another tanker was reported attacked in the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, according to Reuters, while two ships were involved in what was described as an 'accident' in the same area.
sections.whyItMatters: Markets are treating the ceasefire proposal as credible enough to trim risk premiums, but the fundamental supply disruption is worsening — Hormuz traffic has collapsed, and now Bab el-Mandeb is under direct threat. The $88-91 Brent range reflects a market pricing de-escalation hope against multi-chokepoint reality.

sections.heading: Trump Signs Executive Order Tightening Defense Critical Mineral Supply Chains
sections.body:
President Trump signed an executive order on Monday making it harder for U.S. defense contractors to obtain waivers allowing them to buy critical minerals and other materials from China and other prohibited foreign suppliers. White House adviser Peter Navarro said the order ends the approach of 'we tried nothing and we're out of options,' requiring contractors to demonstrate 'exhaustive efforts' to source domestically before any waiver is considered.

Beginning January 1, 2027, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth and military department secretaries will stop issuing waivers unless contractors submit a formal mitigation plan identifying the source of covered materials, documenting exhaustive efforts to find alternatives, and providing a 'strict projected timeline' for removing prohibited materials from their supply chains. The order comes as the Pentagon presses contractors like Boeing and Lockheed Martin to expand domestic weapons production — many missiles, aircraft, and other systems still rely on Chinese suppliers for critical inputs.

The timing is significant: China's customs data released Monday showed zero exports of gallium, dysprosium, terbium, and yttrium to Japan in June, extending a months-long supply disruption. China also sent no yttrium to the United States for the second consecutive month. Beijing controls roughly 90% of global rare earth magnet production and is wielding that dominance selectively — overall rare earth magnet exports rebounded to 5,649 metric tonnes in June from 4,730 in May, but shipments to the U.S. averaged only 479 tonnes per month in the first half of 2026, about 20% below the 2022-2024 average.
sections.whyItMatters: The U.S. is now running two tracks simultaneously on critical minerals: tightening domestic supply chain rules while China continues to weaponize rare earth exports. The 2027 waiver cutoff gives contractors less than 18 months to find alternatives — a timeline that may not be achievable given China's near-monopoly on processing. Japan's manufacturing sector is already feeling the squeeze with zero gallium and heavy rare earth shipments for months.

sections.heading: EU Suspends Methane Law Penalties, Citing Hormuz Disruption
sections.body:
The European Commission on Sunday instructed EU member governments to waive penalties for three years (2027-2029) for oil and gas companies that breach its methane emissions law. The regulation, originally set to take effect in January 2027, would have required all imported oil and gas to meet monitoring, reporting, and verification standards equivalent to EU rules, with fines of up to 20% of annual turnover for non-compliance.

The Commission justified the suspension by citing 'global energy markets tightness caused by the ongoing blockade of the Strait of Hormuz,' which has disrupted transit for a fifth of the world's oil and LNG supply. A group of 17 of the EU's 27 member states, including Germany and the Czech Republic, had requested the delay. The U.S. government and energy exporters had also pressed Brussels to ease the rules.

While the law technically remains on the books, the three-year penalty waiver means foreign suppliers and importers get a grace period. The Commission explicitly stated the move was designed to 'avoid supply disruptions' — a frank admission that climate policy is being subordinated to energy security amid the Gulf crisis.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU's methane regulation was its most ambitious climate law for the energy sector. Suspending it for three years is a clear signal that energy security concerns now outrank climate commitments in Brussels. It also means major LNG and oil suppliers — including those with poor emissions records — face no compliance pressure until 2030, effectively giving a free pass to polluters during a period of supply anxiety.

sections.heading: EU's 21st Russia Sanctions Package Stalls as Bulgaria and Greece Block Key Provisions
sections.body:
The EU's latest sanctions package against Russia — the 21st — has been weakened after Bulgaria vetoed provisions targeting Patriarch Kirill of the Russian Orthodox Church and Vagit Alekperov, founder of Lukoil. Both names were removed from the draft list. Bulgaria's foreign minister, Velislava Petrova-Chamova, argued that sanctioning Kirill risked fueling EU skepticism in Orthodox nations and that sanctions should deliver 'real financial impact rather than serve as symbolic gestures.'

Greece continues to seek exemptions to keep shipping Russian LNG to non-EU countries, arguing that banning transshipment would simply cede market share to competitors. Proposed restrictions on Russian fish and seafood imports were also dropped, and the oil price cap freeze was shortened to one week. The veto baton has effectively passed from Hungary's Viktor Orban to Bulgaria under Prime Minister Rumen Radev — EU unanimity rules mean a single holdout can reshape the entire package.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU's sanctions architecture continues to fray at the edges. Greece's defense of Russian LNG transshipment and Bulgaria's blocking of key designations show that energy dependency and domestic politics still trump collective action. The 21st package will likely pass in diluted form, but the trend is clear: each successive round gets harder to agree and thinner in substance.

sections.heading: Ukraine Hits Moscow Region Oil Depot in 400-Drone Attack
sections.body:
Ukrainian forces launched over 400 drones toward Moscow overnight on July 20, striking an oil depot and logistics facilities in the Moscow region. Large plumes of black smoke rose over the area, and 10 people were wounded. President Zelenskyy confirmed the strikes, saying Ukraine's 'long-range sanctions have produced results against logistics facilities and an oil depot.'

Ukraine's drone chief, Robert Brovdi, reported that forces also struck seven Russian vessels in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov and seven energy facilities in Russian-occupied Crimea. Since July 1, Brovdi said, Ukrainian operations have targeted 183 Russian vessels, 103 energy facilities, and 25 air-defense elements, while reducing the Kerch ferry crossing's capacity by 75%. According to Tenet Research, over 58% of Russia's oil refining capacity is now offline, with refining runs at their lowest since March 2005 at approximately 3.80 million bpd — representing roughly 4.3 million bpd of capacity knocked out across 25 refineries hit since August 2025.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's refining sector is in its worst shape in over two decades. With more than half of capacity offline and the Kerch crossing degraded, Russia's ability to produce and export refined products is severely constrained — which helps explain Moscow's earlier diesel export bans and growing reliance on imported fuel. The energy weapon is cutting both ways.

sections.heading: ASEAN Ministers to Call for Hormuz Reopening at Manila Talks
sections.body:
Southeast Asian foreign ministers meeting in Manila this week are set to express alarm over renewed Middle East hostilities and call for the full reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, according to a draft communique reported by AP. The statement reflects growing anxiety among Asian economies that depend on Gulf oil and LNG transits — Southeast Asian nations are among the most exposed to any prolonged Hormuz disruption, with Singapore, Thailand, and the Philippines all relying heavily on Middle Eastern crude.

The call comes as the Gulf energy crisis enters its fifth month. The Philippines and other ASEAN members have been relatively quiet on the Iran-US conflict, but the Manila statement signals that patience is wearing thin among importing nations that have borne the economic brunt of the disruption.
sections.whyItMatters: ASEAN's collective voice on Hormuz adds diplomatic weight to reopening efforts, but the grouping has no enforcement mechanism. The statement matters more as a signal of how the Gulf crisis is reshaping energy politics in import-dependent Asian economies — and how long those economies are willing to absorb the costs.

bottomLine: The Houthi declaration of a Saudi naval blockade at Bab el-Mandeb opens a second major chokepoint in the same week that Hormuz transit has collapsed to single digits. Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline, its primary Hormuz bypass, now exits into a Red Sea where its own vessels face an embargo — a double bind with no easy workaround. Oil markets are holding steady around $89 on ceasefire hopes, but the physical supply picture is deteriorating. In Washington, Trump's executive order on defense critical minerals is a direct response to China's continued weaponization of rare earth exports, which saw zero gallium, dysprosium, terbium, and yttrium shipments to Japan for another month. In Brussels, the EU is retreating on two fronts: suspending its methane law for three years and diluting its 21st Russia sanctions package. The pattern is consistent — energy security is overriding both climate ambition and sanctions resolve across the Western alliance system.
sources: Reuters, AP, The Telegraph (AP wire), EconoTimes, News18, The National (UAE), RFE/RL, CryptoBriefing/Tenet Research, Bloomberg, Deseret News (White House fact sheet), Firstpost (China customs data), Times Now News, Weekly Blitz, The News (Pakistan), Rystad Energy, ANZ Research
